learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Binary Search, Loops, Time Complexity, Divide and Conquer
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine hiking through mountains. Whenever you are standing on a mountain that is higher than both neighboring mountains, you've reached a peak. The goal is to find any one such peak efficiently without climbing every mountain.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine hiking through mountains. Whenever you are standing on a mountain that is higher than both neighboring mountains, you've reached a peak. The goal is to find any one such peak efficiently without climbing every mountain.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Binary Search pattern whenever the array exhibits a directional property that guarantees an answer exists on one side based on neighboring comparisons.
learning.signals: Whenever comparing neighboring elements tells you which half definitely contains the answer, think of Binary Search on Answer.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: A peak element is an element that is strictly greater than its neighbors. Given a 0-indexed integer array nums, return the index of any peak element. You may imagine that nums[-1] = nums[n] = -∞. The solution must run in O(log n) time.

examples.explanation: nums[2] = 3 is greater than both neighbors.
Index 5 (value 6) is a valid peak. Index 1 is also a valid answer.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Traverse the array and check every element against its neighbors.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of checking every element, observe the slope. If the current element is smaller than the next one, a peak must exist on the right. Otherwise, a peak must exist on the left (including the current element).
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: An increasing slope guarantees a peak on the right, while a decreasing slope guarantees a peak on the left.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever comparing neighboring elements tells you which half definitely contains the answer, think of Binary Search on Answer.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Every comparison removes half of the search space. Since at least one peak always exists, Binary Search guarantees an answer in logarithmic time.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever comparing neighboring elements tells you which half definitely contains the answer, think of Binary Search on Answer.
commonWrongPaths: Using right = mid - 1 instead of right = mid.

hints: Compare the middle element with its next neighbor.
One side is guaranteed to contain a peak.
Think Binary Search instead of Linear Search.
commonMisconceptions: Using right = mid - 1 instead of right = mid.

pattern.name: Binary Search on Answer
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever comparing neighboring elements tells you which half definitely contains the answer, think of Binary Search on Answer.

approaches.title: Linear Scan
approaches.idea: Traverse the array and check every element against its neighbors.

approaches.title: Binary Search
approaches.idea: Use Binary Search by comparing the middle element with its next element.

approaches.title: Binary Search on Slope
approaches.idea: Compare nums[mid] with nums[mid+1]. If nums[mid] is smaller, a peak must exist on the right side. Otherwise, a peak exists on the left side including mid. Continue until both pointers meet.
approaches.intuition: Every comparison removes half of the search space. Since at least one peak always exists, Binary Search guarantees an answer in logarithmic time.
algorithm: If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], move left to mid+1.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,2,3,1]
left=0 right=3
mid=1
2<3 → left=2
left=2 right=3
mid=2
3>1 → right=2
Answer=2

visualization.description: The slope determines which side must contain a peak.

interviewFollowUps: Why does Binary Search work here?
How would you find all peaks?
What if adjacent elements were equal?
How is this related to Mountain Array problems?
interviewTips: Explain why one side always contains a peak.
Move right = mid instead of mid-1.
Remember that any peak is acceptable.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: An increasing slope guarantees a peak on the right, while a decreasing slope guarantees a peak on the left.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Compare Mid & Mid+1 → Move Toward Peak.

importantPoints: At least one peak always exists.
Do not remove mid on descending slope.
commonMistakes: Using right = mid - 1 instead of right = mid.

relatedProblems: Peak Index in a Mountain Array
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
